blocks.content: Julian had been on the porch of his rental when he saw the light go on in Nadia's room and then go off again.
He'd been watching the house — not all of it, just the pattern of its lights. Who was where. Who moved. Who stayed still.

The light in her room had been off for almost an hour. Then on for eight minutes. Then off again. Then he heard the back door of the house open and close and she was on their porch, standing at their railing, looking at the same water he was looking at.
She didn't know he was there. Or if she did, she gave no sign.
He watched her stand at the railing for six minutes. Completely still. Not the stillness of rest — the stillness of someone whose body had been trained to go quiet when their mind needed to work.

Six minutes of standing absolutely still at ten o'clock at night after a day in which a name connected to her friend's debt came out, in which the official word homicide was said out loud, in which everything should be pushing her outward — and she's standing at a railing like she's solving a math problem.
Julian turned to his legal pad.
He wrote: *The circle I keep coming back to. I know what it means. I need to be sure.*
He underlined the last four words.
